<insights>

- High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
- Perfect cohesion (1.0) and zero coupling confirm this zone is fully self-contained — no production code is co-mingled with test files, keeping the test boundary clean.
- The presence of an architecture-policy test file suggests the project enforces structural constraints (e.g. dependency direction, gateway rules) programmatically, which is a strong quality signal.
- With 19 test files covering CI, config, delegation, errors, dev mode, and CLI contracts, this suite provides broad behavioral coverage of the orchestration layer.
- Zero coupling to other zones means these tests spawn real CLI processes rather than importing library code — a correct e2e pattern that avoids false confidence from mocked internals.
- The mix of .test.js and .test.mjs extensions suggests incremental migration toward ESM; standardizing on one extension would reduce tooling friction.
- Architecture-policy tests enforcing dependency rules programmatically are an excellent practice — consider expanding coverage to validate gateway module constraints as the gateway pattern matures.
- The e2e-tests zone and the scripts zone together form a two-layer architectural fitness function strategy: scripts enforce structural constraints at the import-graph level (gateway rules, zone consistency), while e2e-tests enforce behavioral contracts at the process level. This dual-layer approach means violations are caught at two independent checkpoints.
- Because e2e-tests has zero coupling to production zones, the fitness functions themselves cannot be accidentally broken by library refactors — the test suite remains structurally immune to internal churn, which is a strong durability property.
- e2e-tests and scripts zones form complementary fitness function layers: scripts validate import-graph structure, e2e-tests validate process-level behavior. Together they provide full-stack architectural enforcement with no overlap or gap.
- Zero coupling from e2e-tests to any production zone ensures the test suite is structurally immune to internal refactors — a durable design that should be preserved as the codebase grows.
- The 19-file e2e suite covers CLI orchestration exhaustively (ci, config, delegation, errors, dev, contract, arg-contracts) but contains no visible tests for the HTTP MCP server endpoints or web dashboard — a coverage gap that grows in risk as the HTTP transport becomes the recommended MCP path over stdio.
- The architecture-policy test enforces dependency direction at the process level, but because e2e-tests spawns real CLI processes rather than importing code, it cannot assert fine-grained gateway module constraints (e.g. that all cross-package imports pass through a single gateway file) — that validation gap must be owned by the scripts zone instead.
- Mixed .test.js and .test.mjs file extensions within a single test zone creates inconsistent module resolution behavior and tooling friction (e.g. vitest config, coverage collection). Standardize on .test.js (CJS) or .test.mjs (ESM) across the entire e2e-tests zone.
- No e2e tests cover the HTTP MCP server endpoints (rex, sourcevision) or the web dashboard, despite the HTTP transport now being the recommended integration path. Process-level gaps in e2e coverage are high risk because no unit or integration test layer exercises the full HTTP request-response cycle.
- The 414 internal call edges across 19 files (~22 per file) indicate meaningful intra-zone interconnection — likely shared process-spawning helpers defined inline. If this helper logic continues growing, it should be extracted to a tests/e2e/helpers/ subdirectory to prevent individual test files from accumulating setup boilerplate.
- cli-arg-contracts.test.js and cli-contract.test.mjs have semantically overlapping names with no clear boundary between 'arg contracts' and 'contracts' — the distinction between the two files is not self-evident from naming alone, creating a maintenance ambiguity about which file owns which CLI surface.
- The architecture-policy.test.js file sits in the e2e-tests zone but serves the same fitness-function role as scripts/check-*.mjs. If these two enforcement layers check overlapping constraints via different mechanisms (process-spawn vs. import-graph), they can drift out of sync silently — one passing while the other has rotted.
- Rename or split cli-contract.test.mjs and cli-arg-contracts.test.js to make their scope boundaries explicit (e.g. cli-output-contract.test.js vs cli-args-contract.test.js). Overlapping names create ambiguity about which file owns which CLI behavioral surface, making it unclear where to add new contract assertions.
- Extract shared process-spawning and fixture-setup helpers from individual test files into a tests/e2e/helpers/ subdirectory. The 414 internal call edges suggest helper logic is already non-trivial; centralizing it prevents duplication from compounding as new test files are added.
- Audit architecture-policy.test.js against scripts/check-gateway-*.mjs to identify overlapping constraint coverage. Where both layers enforce the same rule, designate one as authoritative and remove the duplicate check to prevent silent divergence between the two enforcement mechanisms.
- [call graph] 414 internal calls, 0 outgoing, 0 incoming (cohesion: 1, coupling: 0)

</insights>
